summary: DeVault draws an extended analogy between the solar system's heliopause and what he calls the 'abiopause' — the turbulent boundary where programming languages interface with C's dominant ABI. He explains how the heliosphere represents the zone of the sun's influence, bounded by the interstellar medium, then maps this onto how every programming language exists as a bubble within a vast sea of C code. The post argues that C's unmatched influence on foundational software forces every other language to maintain a C FFI, creating an awkward boundary layer. He acknowledges the interface is unpleasant for both C enthusiasts and users of other languages, and admits he doesn't know how to fix it.
key_points: C is the single most important programming language because virtually all foundational software infrastructure is written in it
Every language that wants to be useful must support a C FFI, a level of deference no other language commands
The 'abiopause' is the turbulent boundary where a language's idioms meet C's ABI constraints, forcing non-idiomatic code on both sides
The analogy to the heliopause captures how each language's sphere of influence is a small bubble in a vast sea of C code
The boundary is unpleasant regardless of which side you prefer — C users write bad C, and $X users write non-idiomatic $X
money_quotes: In the world of software, the C programming language clearly stands out as the single most important and influential programming language.

Everything forming the critical, foundational parts of your computer is written in it: kernels, drivers, compilers, interpreters, runtimes, hypervisors, databases, libraries, and more are almost all written in C.
No other language has the clout or ubiquity to demand this level of deference from everyone else.
The language's sphere of influence is but a bubble in a sea of C code, and the interface between these two areas of influence is often quite turbulent.
If you like C, you're stuck either writing bad C code or using poorly-suited tools to interface badly with an otherwise good API.
Even if you don't like C, it would be ridiculous to dismiss its influence and importance.
key_insight: Every programming language exists as a small bubble of influence within C's vast dominance, and the boundary where they interface — the 'abiopause' — is inherently turbulent and unpleasant for programmers on both sides.
